metadata.name: Error Recovery
metadata.description: Centralized error recovery definitions for the COOKIE workflow engine. Defines six error types with specific recovery strategies ranging from interactive user prompts to automated retries with exponential backoff. Every protocol in the engine delegates its error handling to the appropriate handler defined here.

general_rules.description: Rules that apply to all error handlers unless overridden by the specific handler definition.
general_rules.rule: All errors are logged to the workflow execution log with: - ISO 8601 timestamp - Error type - Error message - Step ID where the error occurred - Workflow ID - Full stack trace or context (if available)
When an error occurs, the full workflow context at the time of the error is preserved. This enables post-mortem analysis and allows recovery handlers to make informed decisions.
All errors are recorded in the handoff document (handoff.xml) regardless of whether they were recovered. The `recovery_attempted` and `recovery_result` fields track what happened.
For errors that halt the workflow, the user is always notified with a clear, actionable error message. Technical details are available but not the primary display.

If a single workflow step produces multiple errors of the same type, they are grouped and presented together rather than triggering the handler multiple times. This prevents cascading error prompts from overwhelming the user.

handler.name: Missing File
handler.description: Triggered when a file path that was expected to exist on disk cannot be found. This commonly occurs when asset paths are misconfigured, files have been moved or deleted, or a prerequisite workflow step was skipped.

common_causes.cause: Incorrect path in workflow configuration or SSD.
File was moved, renamed, or deleted after workflow was planned.
Prerequisite workflow did not run (e.g., character refs not generated).
Typo in the `ref:` path or variable value.
Project structure does not match expected layout.

step.name: Log the Error
step.action: Record the missing file error with full context: - The resolved absolute path that was not found. - The original path expression before resolution (e.g., `ref:characters/marcus/identity.json`). - The workflow step and input declaration that referenced it. - The loading strategy that was attempted.

step.name: Check Optional Flag
step.action: If the input declaration has `optional: true`: - Log an INFO-level message about the missing optional file. - Set the input value to null in the workflow context. - Continue execution without prompting the user. - Record in the handoff as a non-blocking warning. - EXIT this handler (skip remaining steps).

step.name: Search for Similar Files
step.action: Perform a fuzzy search in the expected directory and parent directories for files with similar names. This helps catch typos and minor path variations. Present any candidates found to the user as suggestions.

step.name: Prompt User for Resolution
Process User Response
step.action:
Based on the user's choice:
Accept the new path from the user. Re-run path resolution and file existence validation. If the new path is valid, update the workflow context and resume. If the new path is also missing, return to step 4.
Mark the input as skipped in the workflow context. Log a WARNING that the input was user-skipped. Resume workflow execution. The step must handle the missing input gracefully (or it will fail with a different error).
Halt the workflow immediately. Produce a handoff document with status "failed" and this error recorded.
Identify the prerequisite workflow that would produce the missing file. Present it to the user for confirmation. If confirmed, queue the prerequisite workflow and suspend the current workflow. Resume after the prerequisite completes.

handler.name: Unresolved Variable
handler.description: Triggered when a `{variable}` placeholder in a workflow step cannot be resolved from any source in the resolution chain. This is always a critical error because it means the workflow configuration is incomplete or incorrect. There is no safe way to proceed with an unresolved variable — guessing would be dangerous (e.g., writing to the wrong path, using the wrong character, processing the wrong scene).

common_causes.cause: Variable was never defined in any config layer.
Typo in the variable name.
Previous step that should have set the variable failed or was skipped.
Config inheritance chain is broken (missing config file).
Variable is defined in episode config but no episode is selected.

step.name: Halt Immediately
step.action: Stop all workflow execution immediately. Do not attempt to continue with partial resolution. Do not substitute defaults or empty strings for unresolved variables.

step.name: Compile Error Report
step.action:
Build a comprehensive error report containing:
CRITICAL ERROR: Unresolved Variable
Variable: {variable_name} Found in: {location_in_step_definition} Step: {step_id} ({step_name}) Workflow: {workflow_name}
Resolution chain checked: 1. Step locals — not found 2. Workflow context — not found 3. Workflow params — not found 4. Episode config — {status: not found / file missing} 5. Project config — {status: not found / file missing} 6. Framework defaults — not found
Possible fixes: - Check spelling: did you mean {similar_variable_name}? - Add "{variable_name}" to your episode config at: {episode_path}/config.yaml - Add "{variable_name}" to your project config at: {project-root}/_cookie/_config/project.yaml - Ensure the step that sets this variable ran successfully before this step.

step.name: Display to User
step.action: Present the full error report to the user. This error requires manual intervention — the user must fix the configuration and re-run the workflow. There is no automated recovery path for unresolved variables.

step.name: Generate Handoff
step.action: Produce a handoff document with: - status: "failed" - The unresolved variable error in the errors array. - Any outputs from steps that completed before the failure. - next_suggested: null (manual fix required).

handler.name: Validation Failure
handler.description: Triggered when data validation fails during preflight checks, dependency graph parsing, config parsing, or output verification. Examples include malformed YAML/JSON, schema violations, invalid dependency graph structures (cycles), and type mismatches.

common_causes.cause: Malformed YAML or JSON in config or SSD files.
Required fields missing from structured data.
Cycle detected in dependency graph.
Type mismatch (string where number expected, etc.).
Schema version incompatibility.
Corrupted file (partial write, encoding issues).

step.name: Log the Validation Error
step.action: Record the full validation error with: - The file or data structure that failed validation. - The specific validation rule that was violated. - The expected format/value vs. the actual format/value. - Line number and position (if applicable for parse errors).

step.name: Attempt Automatic Retry (Once)
step.action:
Retry the validation once. This handles transient issues such as: - File was being written to by another process (partial read). - Temporary filesystem inconsistency. - Race condition with a concurrent workflow.
Before retrying: - Wait 500ms to allow any in-progress writes to complete. - Re-read the file from disk (do not use cached content). - Re-run the validation from scratch.

step.name: Evaluate Retry Result
step.action:
If the retry succeeds: - Log that the validation passed on retry. - Record the transient failure in the handoff as a warning. - Continue workflow execution normally.
If the retry fails: - Proceed to step 4 (halt with details).

step.name: Halt with Detailed Report
Generate Handoff
step.action: Produce a handoff document with status "failed" and the validation failure details in the errors array.

handler.name: API Failure
handler.description: Triggered when an external API call fails. This covers all tool integrations (OpenAI, Runway, ElevenLabs, Suno, etc.) and includes network errors, authentication failures, rate limits, server errors, and timeout issues.

common_causes.cause: Network connectivity issues.
API rate limiting (429 Too Many Requests).
API server errors (500, 502, 503).
Authentication failure (expired API key, invalid credentials).
Request timeout.
API quota exceeded (monthly/daily limit reached).
Malformed API request (bug in request construction).
API service outage or maintenance.

retry_schedule.description: Exponential backoff with three retry attempts. The wait times increase exponentially to avoid overwhelming a struggling API and to wait out transient issues like rate limits.

attempt.description: First retry after 1 second. Handles brief transient errors like momentary network glitches or load balancer hiccups.
Second retry after 4 seconds. Handles rate limiting (most APIs reset their rate limit window within a few seconds) and brief server-side issues.
Third and final retry after 16 seconds. Handles longer server-side recovery periods. If this attempt fails, the error is considered non-transient and requires user intervention.

step.name: Classify the API Error
step.action:
Categorize the error to determine if it is retryable:
- HTTP 429 (Rate Limited) - HTTP 500 (Internal Server Error) - HTTP 502 (Bad Gateway) - HTTP 503 (Service Unavailable) - Connection timeout - Network unreachable (transient) - DNS resolution failure (transient)
- HTTP 400 (Bad Request) — request is malformed, retrying won't help. - HTTP 401 (Unauthorized) — authentication issue, needs config fix. - HTTP 403 (Forbidden) — permission issue, needs config fix. - HTTP 404 (Not Found) — endpoint does not exist. - HTTP 422 (Unprocessable Entity) — input validation error. - API quota exhausted — needs billing or plan change.
For non-retryable errors, skip directly to step 4 (halt).

step.name: Execute Retry with Backoff
step.action:
For retryable errors, execute the retry schedule:
For each attempt (1 through 3): - Wait the specified number of seconds. - Log the retry attempt: "API retry {attempt}/3 for {tool} after {wait}s wait" - Re-execute the API call with identical parameters. - If successful: log recovery, record the transient failure as a warning in the handoff, and continue execution. - If failed: proceed to the next attempt.
Between retries, check if the error classification has changed (e.g., a 429 might become a 401 if the key was revoked during the wait). If the error becomes non-retryable, stop retrying.

step.name: All Retries Exhausted
step.action: If all 3 retry attempts fail: - Log that retry exhaustion occurred. - Record the full API error history (all 4 attempts: initial + 3 retries) with timestamps and error details. - Proceed to step 4.

step.name: Halt with API Error Report
Generate Handoff
step.action: Produce a handoff document with status "failed" (or "partial" if other steps completed). Include the full API error history and all retry attempts in the errors array. Set next_suggested to retry the failed step if the error was transient, or to manual intervention if non-retryable.

handler.name: Cost Exceeded
handler.description: Triggered when a cost threshold is exceeded during cost-gate evaluation or mid-workflow cost tracking. This handler ensures that production costs stay within the creator's budget and that any budget overruns are explicitly acknowledged.

common_causes.cause: Single scene cost exceeds per_scene_halt threshold.
Cumulative session cost exceeds cumulative_halt threshold.
Actual costs during execution significantly exceed estimates.
Multiple retakes consumed the retake buffer and beyond.
Quality tier selection resulted in higher-than-expected costs.

step.name: Halt Immediately
step.action: Stop all billable work immediately. No further API calls or tool invocations that incur cost may proceed until this handler completes with user approval. Non-billable work (local file operations, logging) may continue.

step.name: Generate Cost Report
Present to User and Await Decision
step.action: Display the cost report and wait for the user's decision. This is a blocking prompt — the workflow does not proceed until the user responds.

step.name: Process User Decision
step.action:
Based on the user's choice:
Update the budget limit in the session context. Re-run cost-gate threshold checks with the new limit. If all thresholds now pass, resume workflow execution. Log the budget increase for audit purposes.
Switch the quality tier for remaining work. Re-calculate costs at the new tier. Re-run cost-gate for approval at the new cost.
Suspend the workflow and return control to the user for SSD modifications. Once modifications are saved, re-run dependency-resolve and cost-gate from scratch.
Present a list of remaining scenes/tasks with their costs. Let the user select which ones to skip. Update the execution plan to exclude skipped tasks. Re-calculate costs and re-check thresholds.
Cancel the workflow entirely. Produce a handoff with status "failed" and the cost exceeded error. Include all outputs from steps that completed before the halt.

handler.name: Asset Quality Failure
handler.description: Triggered when a generated asset (image, video, audio) fails an automated quality check but the issue is not severe enough to halt the workflow. The asset is flagged for review at the next quality gate checkpoint rather than stopping production immediately. This allows the pipeline to continue generating other assets while questionable ones are queued for human review.

common_causes.cause: Generated image has low similarity to character reference sheet.
Video clip has visual artifacts or inconsistent motion.
Audio has clipping, distortion, or unexpected silence.
Character consistency check failed (face, clothing, etc.).
Scene composition does not match the SSD specification.
Resolution or aspect ratio does not match requirements.

step.name: Record Quality Failure
step.action: Log the quality failure with full context: - Asset ID and type (image, video, audio). - The quality check that failed (e.g., character consistency, resolution check, artifact detection). - Quality score vs. threshold (if applicable). - The generated asset's file path. - The reference material used for comparison (if applicable). - Screenshot or sample of the quality issue (if capturable).

step.name: Flag the Asset
Continue Workflow Execution
step.action:
DO NOT halt the workflow. The flagged asset is treated as "provisionally accepted" and downstream steps may use it. The flag is carried forward in the workflow context for review at the next quality gate.
Rationale: Halting production for every minor quality issue would make the pipeline extremely slow. Many quality issues are subjective and better evaluated by the creator in batch at a dedicated review checkpoint.

step.name: Quality Gate Review (at next checkpoint)
step.action:
When the workflow reaches a quality-gate step (a dedicated review checkpoint defined in the workflow), all accumulated quality flags are presented to the user:
QUALITY REVIEW — {count} assets flagged
┌─────┬──────────────┬────────────────────┬───────┬──────────┐ │ # │ Asset │ Issue │ Score │ Action │ ├─────┼──────────────┼────────────────────┼───────┼──────────┤ │ 1 │ scene-01.png │ Char. consistency │ 0.62 │ [review] │ │ 2 │ scene-03.mp4 │ Motion artifacts │ 0.71 │ [review] │ │ 3 │ narration.mp3│ Clipping detected │ 0.85 │ [review] │ └─────┴──────────────┴────────────────────┴───────┴──────────┘
For each asset: [accept] — Accept as-is (quality is acceptable) [retake] — Re-generate the asset (costs will apply) [manual] — Replace with a manually provided asset [skip] — Remove this asset from the final output
Process user decisions for each flagged asset: - Accept: clear the quality flag, keep the asset. - Retake: queue re-generation, costs go through cost-gate. - Manual: accept a user-provided file path as replacement. - Skip: remove the asset and adjust the merge step.

step.name: Record in Handoff
step.action:
All quality flags and their review outcomes are recorded in the handoff document. This provides a complete quality audit trail for the production.
Assets that were retaken have their new versions tracked alongside the original flagged versions for comparison.

quality_gate_integration.description: If no explicit quality-gate step exists in the workflow, flagged assets are presented in a summary at the end of the workflow (before the merge step, if one exists). The creator always gets a chance to review flagged assets before they are included in the final output.
quality_gate_integration.fallback_checkpoint: The last non-merge step in the workflow serves as the implicit quality gate if no explicit one is defined.

escalation_matrix.description: Summary of all error types, their recovery behavior, and when they escalate to a workflow halt. Used as a quick reference by the workflow engine.
